instruction: Build a Rust CLI tool using clap that processes log files — supports filtering by date range, log level, and regex pattern, with output in JSON or plain text format.
output:
<think>
For a real-world CLI tool in Rust, clap v4 with derive macros is the standard approach. I need to think about the UX: users want to filter logs by multiple criteria simultaneously, so all filters should be composable (AND logic). Date range parsing needs a good library — chrono is standard. For regex, the `regex` crate is the go-to. Output format selection should be an enum with clap's ValueEnum. Performance matters for log files — I should read line by line with BufReader rather than loading the whole file. For JSON output, serde_json handles serialization. Error handling should use anyhow for the CLI layer since we want nice error messages. One key design decision: should I parse the log format or use regex on raw lines? I'll support a common log format (timestamp + level + message) but also allow raw regex matching. The date parsing is tricky because log timestamps come in many formats — I'll support ISO 8601 and a common syslog-style format.
</think>

```rust
use anyhow::{Context, Result};
use chrono::NaiveDateTime;
use clap::{Parser, ValueEnum};
use regex::Regex;
use serde::Serialize;
use std::fs::File;
use std::io::{BufRead, BufReader, Write};
use std::path::PathBuf;

#[derive(Parser, Debug)]
#[command(name = "logfilter", version, about = "Filter and search log files")]
struct Cli {
    /// Path to the log file
    #[arg(short, long)]
    file: PathBuf,

    /// Minimum log level to include
    #[arg(short, long, value_enum, default_value_t = LogLevel::Debug)]
    level: LogLevel,

    /// Start of date range (ISO 8601: 2024-01-15T00:00:00)
    #[arg(long)]
    after: Option<String>,

    /// End of date range (ISO 8601: 2024-01-16T23:59:59)
    #[arg(long)]
    before: Option<String>,

    /// Regex pattern to match in message body
    #[arg(short, long)]
    pattern: Option<String>,

    /// Output format
    #[arg(short, long, value_enum, default_value_t = OutputFormat::Plain)]
    output: OutputFormat,

    /// Maximum number of matching lines to output
    #[arg(short = 'n', long)]
    max_results: Option<usize>,
}

#[derive(ValueEnum, Clone, Debug, PartialEq, PartialOrd, Serialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "UPPERCASE")]
enum LogLevel {
    Debug,
    Info,
    Warn,
    Error,
    Fatal,
}

impl LogLevel {
    fn from_str_loose(s: &str) -> Option<Self> {
        match s.to_uppercase().as_str() {
            "DEBUG" | "DBG" => Some(Self::Debug),
            "INFO" | "INF" => Some(Self::Info),
            "WARN" | "WARNING" | "WRN" => Some(Self::Warn),
            "ERROR" | "ERR" => Some(Self::Error),
            "FATAL" | "CRIT" | "CRITICAL" => Some(Self::Fatal),
            _ => None,
        }
    }
}

#[derive(ValueEnum, Clone, Debug)]
enum OutputFormat {
    Plain,
    Json,
}

#[derive(Serialize)]
struct LogEntry {
    timestamp: String,
    level: LogLevel,
    message: String,
    line_number: usize,
}

fn parse_log_line(line: &str) -> Option<(String, LogLevel, String)> {
    // Expected: "2024-01-15T10:30:00 INFO Some message here"
    let parts: Vec<&str> = line.splitn(3, ' ').collect();
    if parts.len() < 3 {
        return None;
    }
    let timestamp = parts[0].to_string();
    let level = LogLevel::from_str_loose(parts[1])?;
    let message = parts[2].to_string();
    Some((timestamp, level, message))
}

fn parse_datetime(s: &str) -> Result<NaiveDateTime> {
    NaiveDateTime::parse_from_str(s, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
        .or_else(|_| NaiveDateTime::parse_from_str(s, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"))
        .context(format!("Failed to parse datetime: {s}"))
}

fn main() -> Result<()> {
    let cli = Cli::parse();

    let file = File::open(&cli.file)
        .with_context(|| format!("Cannot open {}", cli.file.display()))?;
    let reader = BufReader::new(file);

    let pattern = cli
        .pattern
        .as_ref()
        .map(|p| Regex::new(p))
        .transpose()
        .context("Invalid regex pattern")?;

    let after = cli.after.as_ref().map(|s| parse_datetime(s)).transpose()?;
    let before = cli.before.as_ref().map(|s| parse_datetime(s)).transpose()?;

    let stdout = std::io::stdout();
    let mut out = stdout.lock();
    let mut count = 0;

    if matches!(cli.output, OutputFormat::Json) {
        write!(out, "[")?;
    }

    let mut first_json = true;

    for (line_num, line_result) in reader.lines().enumerate() {
        let line = line_result?;
        let (timestamp_str, level, message) = match parse_log_line(&line) {
            Some(parsed) => parsed,
            None => continue,
        };

        // Filter by level
        if level < cli.level {
            continue;
        }

        // Filter by date range
        if after.is_some() || before.is_some() {
            if let Ok(ts) = parse_datetime(&timestamp_str) {
                if let Some(ref a) = after {
                    if ts < *a {
                        continue;
                    }
                }
                if let Some(ref b) = before {
                    if ts > *b {
                        continue;
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // Filter by pattern
        if let Some(ref re) = pattern {
            if !re.is_match(&message) {
                continue;
            }
        }

        // Output
        match cli.output {
            OutputFormat::Plain => {
                writeln!(out, "{}", line)?;
            }
            OutputFormat::Json => {
                let entry = LogEntry {
                    timestamp: timestamp_str,
                    level,
                    message,
                    line_number: line_num + 1,
                };
                if !first_json {
                    write!(out, ",")?;
                }
                serde_json::to_writer(&mut out, &entry)?;
                first_json = false;
            }
        }

        count += 1;
        if let Some(max) = cli.max_results {
            if count >= max {
                break;
            }
        }
    }

    if matches!(cli.output, OutputFormat::Json) {
        writeln!(out, "]")?;
    }

    eprintln!("Matched {count} log entries");
    Ok(())
}
```

**Cargo.toml dependencies:**
```toml
[dependencies]
clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive"] }
chrono = "0.4"
regex = "1"
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"
anyhow = "1"
```

instruction: Write a Rust configuration management module using serde that supports layered configs — defaults, config file (TOML), environment variables, and CLI overrides — with validation and helpful error messages.
output:
<think>
Layered configuration is a common real-world pattern. The priority order should be: defaults < config file < env vars < CLI args. In Rust, serde is perfect for deserialization from multiple sources. For TOML, the `toml` crate works well. For env vars, I can use the `envy` crate or manual parsing. The tricky part is merging layers: I need Option<T> fields for each layer so I can tell "not set" from "set to a value". Then I merge from lowest to highest priority. Validation should happen after merging, not per-layer, because a value might only become valid when combined with others. Error messages should tell the user which layer provided the bad value and what's expected. I'll use a builder pattern: ConfigBuilder that accumulates layers and resolves to a final validated Config. For the config file, I should check common paths (./config.toml, ~/.config/app/config.toml, /etc/app/config.toml) with clear messages about which was loaded.
</think>

```rust
use serde::Deserialize;
use std::collections::HashMap;
use std::path::{Path, PathBuf};
use std::{env, fs};
use thiserror::Error;

#[derive(Error, Debug)]
pub enum ConfigError {
    #[error("Config file not found: {0}")]
    FileNotFound(PathBuf),
    #[error("Failed to parse config file {path}: {source}")]
    ParseError {
        path: PathBuf,
        source: toml::de::Error,
    },
    #[error("Validation error: {field} — {message} (source: {source_layer})")]
    Validation {
        field: String,
        message: String,
        source_layer: String,
    },
    #[error("Environment variable {var} has invalid value '{value}': {reason}")]
    EnvVarError {
        var: String,
        value: String,
        reason: String,
    },
    #[error("IO error: {0}")]
    Io(#[from] std::io::Error),
}

/// Partial config — all fields optional for layered merging.
#[derive(Deserialize, Default, Debug, Clone)]
pub struct PartialConfig {
    pub host: Option<String>,
    pub port: Option<u16>,
    pub database_url: Option<String>,
    pub log_level: Option<String>,
    pub max_connections: Option<u32>,
    pub tls_cert_path: Option<String>,
    pub tls_key_path: Option<String>,
    pub request_timeout_secs: Option<u64>,
    pub cors_origins: Option<Vec<String>>,
}

/// Final validated config — all required fields present.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct AppConfig {
    pub host: String,
    pub port: u16,
    pub database_url: String,
    pub log_level: String,
    pub max_connections: u32,
    pub tls_cert_path: Option<String>,
    pub tls_key_path: Option<String>,
    pub request_timeout_secs: u64,
    pub cors_origins: Vec<String>,
}

impl PartialConfig {
    fn merge(self, other: PartialConfig) -> PartialConfig {
        PartialConfig {
            host: other.host.or(self.host),
            port: other.port.or(self.port),
            database_url: other.database_url.or(self.database_url),
            log_level: other.log_level.or(self.log_level),
            max_connections: other.max_connections.or(self.max_connections),
            tls_cert_path: other.tls_cert_path.or(self.tls_cert_path),
            tls_key_path: other.tls_key_path.or(self.tls_key_path),
            request_timeout_secs: other.request_timeout_secs.or(self.request_timeout_secs),
            cors_origins: other.cors_origins.or(self.cors_origins),
        }
    }
}

pub struct ConfigBuilder {
    layers: Vec<(String, PartialConfig)>,
    env_prefix: String,
}

impl ConfigBuilder {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        let defaults = PartialConfig {
            host: Some("127.0.0.1".into()),
            port: Some(8080),
            log_level: Some("info".into()),
            max_connections: Some(100),
            request_timeout_secs: Some(30),
            cors_origins: Some(vec![]),
            ..Default::default()
        };
        Self {
            layers: vec![("defaults".into(), defaults)],
            env_prefix: "APP_".into(),
        }
    }

    pub fn env_prefix(mut self, prefix: &str) -> Self {
        self.env_prefix = prefix.to_string();
        self
    }

    pub fn with_file(mut self, path: &Path) -> Result<Self, ConfigError> {
        if !path.exists() {
            return Err(ConfigError::FileNotFound(path.to_path_buf()));
        }
        let content = fs::read_to_string(path)?;
        let partial: PartialConfig = toml::from_str(&content).map_err(|e| {
            ConfigError::ParseError {
                path: path.to_path_buf(),
                source: e,
            }
        })?;
        self.layers
            .push((format!("file:{}", path.display()), partial));
        Ok(self)
    }

    pub fn with_optional_file(self, path: &Path) -> Result<Self, ConfigError> {
        if path.exists() {
            self.with_file(path)
        } else {
            Ok(self)
        }
    }

    pub fn with_env(mut self) -> Result<Self, ConfigError> {
        let p = &self.env_prefix;
        let mut partial = PartialConfig::default();

        if let Ok(v) = env::var(format!("{p}HOST")) {
            partial.host = Some(v);
        }
        if let Ok(v) = env::var(format!("{p}PORT")) {
            partial.port = Some(v.parse().map_err(|_| ConfigError::EnvVarError {
                var: format!("{p}PORT"),
                value: v,
                reason: "expected u16".into(),
            })?);
        }
        if let Ok(v) = env::var(format!("{p}DATABASE_URL")) {
            partial.database_url = Some(v);
        }
        if let Ok(v) = env::var(format!("{p}LOG_LEVEL")) {
            partial.log_level = Some(v);
        }
        if let Ok(v) = env::var(format!("{p}MAX_CONNECTIONS")) {
            partial.max_connections = Some(v.parse().map_err(|_| ConfigError::EnvVarError {
                var: format!("{p}MAX_CONNECTIONS"),
                value: v,
                reason: "expected u32".into(),
            })?);
        }

        self.layers.push(("environment".into(), partial));
        Ok(self)
    }

    pub fn with_overrides(mut self, overrides: PartialConfig) -> Self {
        self.layers.push(("cli_overrides".into(), overrides));
        self
    }

    pub fn build(self) -> Result<AppConfig, ConfigError> {
        let mut merged = PartialConfig::default();
        let mut field_sources: HashMap<String, String> = HashMap::new();

        for (layer_name, partial) in self.layers {
            if partial.host.is_some() { field_sources.insert("host".into(), layer_name.clone()); }
            if partial.port.is_some() { field_sources.insert("port".into(), layer_name.clone()); }
            if partial.database_url.is_some() { field_sources.insert("database_url".into(), layer_name.clone()); }
            merged = merged.merge(partial);
        }

        let config = AppConfig {
            host: merged.host.ok_or_else(|| ConfigError::Validation {
                field: "host".into(), message: "required".into(),
                source_layer: "none".into(),
            })?,
            port: merged.port.unwrap_or(8080),
            database_url: merged.database_url.ok_or_else(|| ConfigError::Validation {
                field: "database_url".into(),
                message: "required — set APP_DATABASE_URL or add to config file".into(),
                source_layer: "none".into(),
            })?,
            log_level: merged.log_level.unwrap_or_else(|| "info".into()),
            max_connections: merged.max_connections.unwrap_or(100),
            tls_cert_path: merged.tls_cert_path,
            tls_key_path: merged.tls_key_path,
            request_timeout_secs: merged.request_timeout_secs.unwrap_or(30),
            cors_origins: merged.cors_origins.unwrap_or_default(),
        };

        // Validation
        let valid_levels = ["trace", "debug", "info", "warn", "error"];
        if !valid_levels.contains(&config.log_level.to_lowercase().as_str()) {
            return Err(ConfigError::Validation {
                field: "log_level".into(),
                message: format!("must be one of {:?}, got '{}'", valid_levels, config.log_level),
                source_layer: field_sources.get("log_level").cloned().unwrap_or("unknown".into()),
            });
        }

        if config.tls_cert_path.is_some() != config.tls_key_path.is_some() {
            return Err(ConfigError::Validation {
                field: "tls".into(),
                message: "both tls_cert_path and tls_key_path must be set, or neither".into(),
                source_layer: "merged".into(),
            });
        }

        Ok(config)
    }
}

fn main() -> Result<(), ConfigError> {
    let config = ConfigBuilder::new()
        .env_prefix("MYAPP_")
        .with_optional_file(Path::new("config.toml"))?
        .with_env()?
        .build()?;

    println!("Server: {}:{}", config.host, config.port);
    println!("DB: {}", config.database_url);
    Ok(())
}
```

instruction: Implement graceful shutdown in a Rust tokio application that manages multiple async tasks — an HTTP server, a background job processor, and a metrics reporter — ensuring all in-flight work completes.
output:
<think>
Graceful shutdown in async Rust is one of the most practical challenges developers face. The key components: a shutdown signal (typically SIGTERM/SIGINT via tokio::signal), a broadcast mechanism to notify all tasks, and a way to wait for completion with a timeout. I'll use tokio::sync::watch for the shutdown signal since it's efficient for one-writer-many-readers. Each subsystem needs to be cancellation-aware — checking the shutdown signal in its main loop. The HTTP server (using axum or hyper) has its own graceful shutdown mechanism. Background jobs need to finish their current item but not pick up new ones. The metrics reporter should flush one final time. I need a timeout on the overall shutdown — if tasks don't stop within N seconds, force-exit. The pattern is: signal → notify all → wait with timeout → force. I should use JoinSet to manage all the spawned tasks and track their completion.
</think>

```rust
use std::time::Duration;
use tokio::sync::watch;
use tokio::signal;
use tokio::time;

/// Shared shutdown signal — watch channel is ideal for fan-out.
struct ShutdownController {
    tx: watch::Sender<bool>,
    rx: watch::Receiver<bool>,
}

impl ShutdownController {
    fn new() -> Self {
        let (tx, rx) = watch::channel(false);
        Self { tx, rx }
    }

    fn trigger(&self) {
        let _ = self.tx.send(true);
    }

    fn subscribe(&self) -> ShutdownSignal {
        ShutdownSignal {
            rx: self.rx.clone(),
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Clone)]
struct ShutdownSignal {
    rx: watch::Receiver<bool>,
}

impl ShutdownSignal {
    async fn wait(&mut self) {
        let _ = self.rx.wait_for(|&v| v).await;
    }

    fn is_shutdown(&self) -> bool {
        *self.rx.borrow()
    }
}

/// HTTP server subsystem
async fn run_http_server(mut shutdown: ShutdownSignal) {
    use axum::{routing::get, Router};

    let app = Router::new()
        .route("/health", get(|| async { "ok" }))
        .route("/api/data", get(|| async { "[{\"id\": 1}]" }));

    let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:8080")
        .await
        .expect("Failed to bind");

    println!("[http] Listening on :8080");

    axum::serve(listener, app)
        .with_graceful_shutdown(async move {
            shutdown.wait().await;
            println!("[http] Shutting down gracefully...");
        })
        .await
        .expect("Server error");

    println!("[http] All connections drained");
}

/// Background job processor
async fn run_job_processor(mut shutdown: ShutdownSignal) {
    println!("[jobs] Starting job processor");

    loop {
        if shutdown.is_shutdown() {
            println!("[jobs] Shutdown received, stopping after current batch");
            break;
        }

        // Simulate picking up and processing a job
        tokio::select! {
            _ = async {
                // Simulate job processing
                println!("[jobs] Processing job...");
                time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(2)).await;
                println!("[jobs] Job complete");
            } => {}
            _ = shutdown.wait() => {
                // Let current job logic handle its own cleanup
                println!("[jobs] Interrupted — finishing current job");
                // In real code, the job would have its own
                // checkpointing/completion logic here.
                break;
            }
        }

        // Brief pause between jobs
        time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(100)).await;
    }

    println!("[jobs] Processor stopped cleanly");
}

/// Metrics reporter — periodic flush
async fn run_metrics_reporter(mut shutdown: ShutdownSignal) {
    let mut interval = time::interval(Duration::from_secs(15));
    let mut pending_metrics: Vec<String> = Vec::new();

    println!("[metrics] Reporter started");

    loop {
        tokio::select! {
            _ = interval.tick() => {
                pending_metrics.push(format!("metric_at_{}", chrono::Utc::now()));
                flush_metrics(&mut pending_metrics).await;
            }
            _ = shutdown.wait() => {
                println!("[metrics] Final flush...");
                flush_metrics(&mut pending_metrics).await;
                break;
            }
        }
    }

    println!("[metrics] Reporter stopped");
}

async fn flush_metrics(metrics: &mut Vec<String>) {
    if metrics.is_empty() {
        return;
    }
    println!("[metrics] Flushing {} metrics", metrics.len());
    // In real code: HTTP POST to metrics service
    time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(100)).await;
    metrics.clear();
}

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    let shutdown = ShutdownController::new();

    // Spawn all subsystems
    let http_handle = tokio::spawn(run_http_server(shutdown.subscribe()));
    let jobs_handle = tokio::spawn(run_job_processor(shutdown.subscribe()));
    let metrics_handle = tokio::spawn(run_metrics_reporter(shutdown.subscribe()));

    // Wait for shutdown signal
    tokio::select! {
        _ = signal::ctrl_c() => {
            println!("\n[main] Received Ctrl+C");
        }
        _ = async {
            #[cfg(unix)]
            {
                let mut term = signal::unix::signal(signal::unix::SignalKind::terminate())
                    .expect("Failed to register SIGTERM");
                term.recv().await;
            }
            #[cfg(not(unix))]
            std::future::pending::<()>().await;
        } => {
            println!("[main] Received SIGTERM");
        }
    }

    // Trigger graceful shutdown
    println!("[main] Initiating graceful shutdown...");
    shutdown.trigger();

    // Wait for all subsystems with a hard timeout
    let shutdown_timeout = Duration::from_secs(30);
    let result = time::timeout(
        shutdown_timeout,
        async {
            let _ = tokio::join!(http_handle, jobs_handle, metrics_handle);
        },
    )
    .await;

    match result {
        Ok(_) => println!("[main] All subsystems stopped gracefully"),
        Err(_) => {
            eprintln!("[main] Shutdown timed out after {shutdown_timeout:?}, forcing exit");
            std::process::exit(1);
        }
    }

    println!("[main] Clean exit");
}
```

instruction: Write a Rust module for cross-platform file path handling that normalizes paths, resolves symlinks safely, and provides utilities for working with both Unix and Windows paths in a platform-agnostic way.
output:
<think>
Cross-platform path handling in Rust sounds simple because std::path::Path exists, but real-world code needs more. The main challenges: normalizing paths (removing `.`, `..`, redundant separators) without filesystem access (lexical normalization) vs with filesystem access (canonicalize, which resolves symlinks). Symlink resolution needs to be safe — avoid symlink attacks where a symlink points outside an allowed directory (path traversal). I should provide both lexical normalization (fast, no I/O) and safe canonicalization (with I/O, with jail checking). The jail check ensures the resolved path stays within an allowed directory — critical for file servers and sandboxes. I also need utilities like finding relative paths between two absolute paths, extending paths safely, and checking if a path is safe for use in both Unix and Windows (no reserved names like CON, PRN, no invalid chars). This is the kind of utility module every serious Rust project needs.
</think>

```rust
use std::path::{Path, PathBuf, Component};
use std::io;

/// Errors from path operations
#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
pub enum PathError {
    #[error("Path escapes jail directory: resolved to {resolved}, jail is {jail}")]
    JailEscape { resolved: String, jail: String },

    #[error("Path contains invalid component: {0}")]
    InvalidComponent(String),

    #[error("Path contains Windows-reserved name: {0}")]
    ReservedName(String),

    #[error("IO error: {0}")]
    Io(#[from] io::Error),
}

/// Lexical normalization — no filesystem access.
/// Resolves `.` and `..` components, collapses separators.
/// Does NOT resolve symlinks.
pub fn normalize(path: &Path) -> PathBuf {
    let mut result = PathBuf::new();

    for component in path.components() {
        match component {
            Component::CurDir => {}
            Component::ParentDir => {
                if !result.pop() {
                    // Can't go above root — keep the ..
                    result.push("..");
                }
            }
            other => result.push(other),
        }
    }

    if result.as_os_str().is_empty() {
        result.push(".");
    }

    result
}

/// Safe canonicalization — resolves symlinks but ensures
/// the result stays within `jail_dir`.
pub fn safe_canonicalize(path: &Path, jail_dir: &Path) -> Result<PathBuf, PathError> {
    let jail_canonical = jail_dir.canonicalize()?;
    let resolved = path.canonicalize()?;

    if !resolved.starts_with(&jail_canonical) {
        return Err(PathError::JailEscape {
            resolved: resolved.display().to_string(),
            jail: jail_canonical.display().to_string(),
        });
    }

    Ok(resolved)
}

/// Join a path within a jail directory safely.
/// Prevents directory traversal via `..` or absolute paths.
pub fn safe_join(base: &Path, user_input: &Path) -> Result<PathBuf, PathError> {
    // Reject absolute paths in user input
    if user_input.is_absolute() {
        return Err(PathError::InvalidComponent(
            "absolute paths not allowed".into(),
        ));
    }

    // Normalize to catch .. traversal
    let normalized = normalize(user_input);

    // Check for remaining ..
    for component in normalized.components() {
        if matches!(component, Component::ParentDir) {
            return Err(PathError::InvalidComponent(
                "parent directory traversal not allowed".into(),
            ));
        }
    }

    Ok(base.join(normalized))
}

/// Windows-reserved device names that are invalid as filenames.
const WINDOWS_RESERVED: &[&str] = &[
    "CON", "PRN", "AUX", "NUL",
    "COM1", "COM2", "COM3", "COM4", "COM5", "COM6", "COM7", "COM8", "COM9",
    "LPT1", "LPT2", "LPT3", "LPT4", "LPT5", "LPT6", "LPT7", "LPT8", "LPT9",
];

/// Characters invalid in Windows filenames.
const WINDOWS_INVALID_CHARS: &[char] = &['<', '>', ':', '"', '|', '?', '*'];

/// Check if a filename is safe on both Unix and Windows.
pub fn is_portable_filename(name: &str) -> Result<(), PathError> {
    if name.is_empty() || name == "." || name == ".." {
        return Err(PathError::InvalidComponent(name.into()));
    }

    // Check Windows reserved names (case-insensitive, with or without extension)
    let upper = name.to_uppercase();
    let stem = upper.split('.').next().unwrap_or(&upper);
    if WINDOWS_RESERVED.contains(&stem) {
        return Err(PathError::ReservedName(name.into()));
    }

    // Check invalid characters
    for ch in name.chars() {
        if ch.is_control() || WINDOWS_INVALID_CHARS.contains(&ch) {
            return Err(PathError::InvalidComponent(format!(
                "invalid character '{}' in filename",
                ch
            )));
        }
    }

    // Trailing dots/spaces invalid on Windows
    if name.ends_with('.') || name.ends_with(' ') {
        return Err(PathError::InvalidComponent(
            "trailing dot or space not allowed".into(),
        ));
    }

    Ok(())
}

/// Compute relative path from `base` to `target`.
pub fn relative_path(base: &Path, target: &Path) -> Option<PathBuf> {
    let base_norm = normalize(base);
    let target_norm = normalize(target);

    let base_components: Vec<_> = base_norm.components().collect();
    let target_components: Vec<_> = target_norm.components().collect();

    // Find common prefix length
    let common = base_components
        .iter()
        .zip(target_components.iter())
        .take_while(|(a, b)| a == b)
        .count();

    if common == 0 {
        return None;
    }

    let mut result = PathBuf::new();
    for _ in common..base_components.len() {
        result.push("..");
    }
    for comp in &target_components[common..] {
        result.push(comp);
    }

    Some(result)
}

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_normalize() {
        assert_eq!(normalize(Path::new("a/b/../c")), PathBuf::from("a/c"));
        assert_eq!(normalize(Path::new("a/./b/./c")), PathBuf::from("a/b/c"));
        assert_eq!(normalize(Path::new("./")), PathBuf::from("."));
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_safe_join_blocks_traversal() {
        let base = Path::new("/srv/data");
        assert!(safe_join(base, Path::new("../../etc/passwd")).is_err());
        assert!(safe_join(base, Path::new("/etc/passwd")).is_err());
        assert!(safe_join(base, Path::new("users/profile.json")).is_ok());
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_portable_filename() {
        assert!(is_portable_filename("readme.txt").is_ok());
        assert!(is_portable_filename("CON").is_err());
        assert!(is_portable_filename("file<name").is_err());
        assert!(is_portable_filename("trailing.").is_err());
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_relative_path() {
        let rel = relative_path(Path::new("/a/b/c"), Path::new("/a/d/e")).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(rel, PathBuf::from("../../d/e"));
    }
}
```

instruction: Create a Rust module for a CLI tool that generates project scaffolding from templates, using clap for argument parsing, walkdir for directory traversal, and handlebars-style variable substitution in file contents and names.
output:
<think>
Project scaffolding is a classic CLI tool challenge. The core workflow: read a template directory tree, process each file by replacing {{variable}} placeholders in both file contents and file/directory names, write the result to the output directory. Key design decisions: template format — I'll use a simple {{key}} syntax processed with regex rather than pulling in the full handlebars crate for this use case. Variable sources: CLI args and an optional config file. I need to handle binary files properly — don't try to do string replacement on images or compiled files. I'll detect binary files by checking for null bytes in the first 8KB. Directory creation needs to be recursive and handle the case where the output dir already exists (prompt or error). File name substitution is important — template might have a file called `{{project_name}}.rs` that becomes `my_app.rs`. Edge cases: nested template variables in paths, empty files, symlinks in template dirs.
</think>

```rust
use clap::Parser;
use regex::Regex;
use std::collections::HashMap;
use std::fs;
use std::io::Read;
use std::path::{Path, PathBuf};
use walkdir::WalkDir;

#[derive(Parser, Debug)]
#[command(name = "scaffold", about = "Generate project from template")]
struct Cli {
    /// Template directory path
    #[arg(short, long)]
    template: PathBuf,

    /// Output directory
    #[arg(short, long)]
    output: PathBuf,

    /// Variables as key=value pairs
    #[arg(short = 'v', long = "var", value_parser = parse_var)]
    vars: Vec<(String, String)>,

    /// Overwrite existing output directory
    #[arg(long, default_value_t = false)]
    force: bool,

    /// Dry run — print what would be created
    #[arg(long, default_value_t = false)]
    dry_run: bool,
}

fn parse_var(s: &str) -> Result<(String, String), String> {
    let parts: Vec<&str> = s.splitn(2, '=').collect();
    if parts.len() != 2 {
        return Err(format!("Expected KEY=VALUE, got '{s}'"));
    }
    Ok((parts[0].to_string(), parts[1].to_string()))
}

struct TemplateEngine {
    vars: HashMap<String, String>,
    pattern: Regex,
}

impl TemplateEngine {
    fn new(vars: Vec<(String, String)>) -> Self {
        Self {
            vars: vars.into_iter().collect(),
            pattern: Regex::new(r"\{\{\s*(\w+)\s*\}\}").unwrap(),
        }
    }

    /// Substitute {{var}} in a string, returning (result, list of unknown vars).
    fn substitute(&self, input: &str) -> (String, Vec<String>) {
        let mut unknown = Vec::new();
        let result = self.pattern.replace_all(input, |caps: &regex::Captures| {
            let key = &caps[1];
            match self.vars.get(key) {
                Some(val) => val.clone(),
                None => {
                    unknown.push(key.to_string());
                    caps[0].to_string()
                }
            }
        });
        (result.into_owned(), unknown)
    }

    /// Check if file content appears to be binary.
    fn is_binary(content: &[u8]) -> bool {
        let check_len = content.len().min(8192);
        content[..check_len].contains(&0)
    }
}

fn scaffold(cli: &Cli) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    let engine = TemplateEngine::new(cli.vars.clone());
    let template_root = cli.template.canonicalize()?;

    if cli.output.exists() && !cli.force {
        return Err(format!(
            "Output directory '{}' already exists. Use --force to overwrite.",
            cli.output.display()
        )
        .into());
    }

    // Collect all unknown variables first (dry-run validation)
    let mut all_unknown: Vec<String> = Vec::new();
    let mut plan: Vec<(PathBuf, PathBuf, bool)> = Vec::new(); // (src, dest, is_dir)

    for entry in WalkDir::new(&template_root).follow_links(false) {
        let entry = entry?;
        let src_path = entry.path();
        let relative = src_path.strip_prefix(&template_root)?;

        // Substitute variables in the relative path
        let relative_str = relative.to_string_lossy();
        let (substituted_path, path_unknown) = engine.substitute(&relative_str);
        all_unknown.extend(path_unknown);

        let dest_path = cli.output.join(&substituted_path);

        if entry.file_type().is_dir() {
            plan.push((src_path.to_path_buf(), dest_path, true));
        } else {
            plan.push((src_path.to_path_buf(), dest_path, false));
        }
    }

    // Check for unknown variables
    all_unknown.sort();
    all_unknown.dedup();
    if !all_unknown.is_empty() {
        eprintln!("Warning: unknown template variables: {:?}", all_unknown);
        eprintln!("Available variables: {:?}", engine.vars.keys().collect::<Vec<_>>());
    }

    // Execute plan
    let mut files_created = 0;
    let mut dirs_created = 0;

    for (src, dest, is_dir) in &plan {
        if *is_dir {
            if cli.dry_run {
                println!("[dir]  {}", dest.display());
            } else {
                fs::create_dir_all(dest)?;
            }
            dirs_created += 1;
        } else {
            if cli.dry_run {
                println!("[file] {}", dest.display());
            } else {
                // Ensure parent exists
                if let Some(parent) = dest.parent() {
                    fs::create_dir_all(parent)?;
                }

                let mut content = Vec::new();
                fs::File::open(src)?.read_to_end(&mut content)?;

                if TemplateEngine::is_binary(&content) {
                    // Copy binary files as-is
                    fs::write(dest, &content)?;
                } else {
                    // Text file — substitute variables
                    let text = String::from_utf8_lossy(&content);
                    let (processed, _) = engine.substitute(&text);
                    fs::write(dest, processed)?;
                }
            }
            files_created += 1;
        }
    }

    let action = if cli.dry_run { "Would create" } else { "Created" };
    println!(
        "{action}: {dirs_created} directories, {files_created} files in {}",
        cli.output.display()
    );

    Ok(())
}

fn main() {
    let cli = Cli::parse();
    if let Err(e) = scaffold(&cli) {
        eprintln!("Error: {e}");
        std::process::exit(1);
    }
}
```

**Usage:**
```bash
# Create project from template with variable substitution
scaffold --template ./templates/rust-web \
         --output ./my-project \
         -v project_name=my_project \
         -v author="Jane Doe" \
         -v license=MIT

# Dry run to see what would be created
scaffold --template ./templates/rust-web --output ./test --dry-run \
         -v project_name=test
```
